one_line_takeaway: Reanalyzing the S&P 2023 SEM Trojan-detection dataset with a new via-position similarity metric shows that 'invisible inversion' cell pairs (e.g., XOR/XNOR, BUF/INV) are an intrinsic, and at 28nm markedly worsening, property of standard cell libraries -- not merely an imaging artifact -- enabling undetectable privilege-escalation Trojans that can nonetheless be excluded from synthesis at under 0.05% overhead.
key_finding: Trojanizability is largely an intrinsic property of the cell library rather than an artifact of image quality: mean similarity scores were nearly identical across the 90nm (0.35), 65nm (0.373), and 40nm (0.37) nodes but shifted sharply higher to 0.5 at 28nm, with 28nm containing five functionally distinct cell-type pairs that are perfectly indistinguishable (similarity score of 1) by via pattern -- versus one such pair at 90nm, one at 40nm (tie-high/tie-low), and zero at 65nm. These near-identical pairs recur across all four nodes predominantly as logical inversions ('invisible inversions'): XOR vs. XNOR, BUF vs. INV, and TIEL vs. TIEH. On the Trojan-detection task, the authors' via-position (VP) method achieved zero false negatives across all four nodes overall and reduced false positives in three of four nodes versus Puschner et al.'s TM/VM baselines, but performed worse specifically at 65nm, where bright SEM imaging artifacts are frequently misclassified as vias (elevating VP false positives there). Instances of the most-similar (score-1) pairs remained undetectable by all methods, including VP, confirming an information-theoretic detection floor for those specific cell pairs. In the Ibex case study, the privilege-escalation Trojans (3 XNOR-XOR swaps at 65nm; 2 INV-BUF swaps at 28nm) required no re-routing since substituted cells share identical lowest-metal-layer via patterns/connections with the originals, and excluding the five vulnerable 28nm pairs from synthesis via dont_use constraints cost only 0.013% area, 0.047% timing, and 0.022% power overhead.
core_contribution: The paper reanalyzes the public IEEE S&P 2023 (Puschner et al.) backside-SEM Trojan-detection dataset (90nm, 65nm, 40nm, 28nm digital ICs) to determine whether that dataset's observed detection failures at smaller nodes are caused by imaging-quality degradation or by an intrinsic property of the standard cell libraries themselves. The authors introduce a new, deterministic, threshold-free 'Trojanizability' similarity metric based on aligning and clustering via positions across 50 randomly sampled instances per cell type (via alignment by exhaustive translation search, per-via majority voting, k-means clustering to derive a robust cell-type 'representative', then Jaccard-distance-style via-set similarity scoring), and apply it exhaustively to all functionally distinct, equal-width cell-type pairs in all four node libraries. They then build a Trojan classifier from this metric and compare its false-negative (beta-error) performance against Puschner et al.'s original template-matching (TM) and via-mask-matching (VM) baselines on the same dataset. Finally, as a concrete demonstration, they design (but do not fabricate) two silicon-level privilege-escalation Trojans in an Ibex RISC-V core (65nm: 3 XNOR-XOR substitutions; 28nm: 2 INV-BUF substitutions) that force the privilege-check logic to always evaluate true, and they quantify the synthesis-time cost of excluding the vulnerable cell pairs via EDA dont_use constraints.
threat_model: Adversary operates in the post-design, pre-fabrication supply chain: the chip design leaving the design house is assumed benign (no untrusted employees, subverted EDA tools, or malicious IP blocks considered). The attacker gains the ability to modify design data, masks, or production inputs during hand-off to or processing by an outsourced foundry (e.g., an interceptor of GDSII files, a compromised foundry file-storage/submission portal, or a single foundry employee with file access) and is restricted to 'cell substitution attacks': replacing one standard cell from the target library/PDK with a different, functionally distinct, same-width cell from the same library. This preserves nominal process/mask characteristics so the substitution requires no deviation from normal foundry mask-creation or process steps. Fully unrestricted foundry adversaries (e.g., doping-level modifications) are explicitly excluded as intractable to defend against. Detection capability (blue team) is assumed equivalent to Puschner et al.: human analysts aided by SEM imaging and automated image-matching over the same published backside SEM imagery and anonymized layout data; electrical/functional/side-channel testing is explicitly out of scope for the visual analysis (acknowledged as a limitation and complementary defense).
limitations: Findings are explicitly scoped to visual/backside-SEM indistinguishability under the specific imagery and image-processing primitives used; the authors state this does not imply a fundamental impossibility of distinguishing the cells under all conceivable imaging conditions (e.g., dopant-contrast or front-side delayering imaging might disambiguate some pairs, but such techniques are costly/operationally challenging and out of scope). The threat model excludes wafer-processing/doping-level and mask-alignment attacks not reducible to same-library cell substitution, and excludes design-stage insider threats. The analysis is purely visual and does not evaluate whether electrical testing or power/EM side-channel measurement would detect the substitutions (acknowledged as future work). The Ibex Trojans were designed and synthesized but never fabricated or physically imaged/tested; the paper reuses existing dataset SEM images by analogy rather than presenting new hardware evidence. The overall study is limited to four standard cell libraries from one dataset, though the authors express optimism the method generalizes to other libraries/nodes.

datasets_used: Public S&P 2023 (Puschner et al.) backside SEM image + anonymized GDSII dataset spanning four digital IC designs fabricated at 90nm, 65nm, 40nm, and 28nm technology nodes
quantitative_results: 28nm node contains 5 functionally distinct cell-type pairs with similarity score of 1 (perfectly indistinguishable via patterns); 90nm and 40nm each contain 1 such pair; 65nm contains none
Ibex RISC-V privilege-escalation Trojan: 3 XNOR-XOR substitutions at 65nm, 2 INV-BUF substitutions at 28nm
Mitigation cost of excluding the five indistinguishable 28nm cell pairs (9 cell types, 72 dont_use constraints): 0.013% area, 0.047% timing, 0.022% power overhead

Via Position (VP) method: zero false negatives across all four nodes on the original Puschner et al. detection challenge; reduced false positives vs. baselines in 3 of 4 nodes, underperforming specifically at 65nm
Less than 10% of computed cell-type representatives required manual correction during validation

Example similarity scores cited for specific ranked pairs: 90nm 5th/10th ranked pairs score 0.87/0.8; 65nm 1st/5th ranked pairs score 0.97/0.92, 10th ranked (BUF-NOR) 0.91; 40nm 5th ranked (BUF-INV) 0.83, 10th ranked (INV-NOR) 0.77; 28nm 10th ranked pair score 0.99
baselines_compared: Template matching (TM) -- Puschner et al.'s S&P 2023 method
Via-mask matching (VM) -- Puschner et al.'s S&P 2023 method
